title: Monthly sea ice extent in the Arctic, decadal average
subtitle: Each point represents the monthly average sea ice extent, averaged across all years within the decade. The current decade is highlighted in red, with the current year shown in black for comparison.
caption:
Monthly sea ice extent in the Arctic, decadal average

Each point represents the monthly average sea ice extent, averaged across all years within the decade. The current decade is highlighted in red, with the current year shown in black for comparison.

The chart displays monthly sea ice extent in the Arctic across five decadal averages and the current year, plotted against months 1 through 12 on the horizontal axis. The vertical axis ranges from 4 to 16 million km². The 1980s line, shown in lightest blue, remains highest across all months, peaking near 15.8 million km² in month 3 and dipping to approximately 7.2 million km² in month 9. Each subsequent decade—1990s, 2000s, 2010s, and 2020s—shows progressively lower values, with the 2020s line in red consistently below prior decades except for a slight convergence with the 2010s around months 10 to 12. The black line representing 2025 tracks below all decadal averages for most of the year, reaching its lowest point near 4.5 million km² in month 9, then rising to about 11.2 million km² by month 12. All lines follow a similar seasonal pattern: increasing from month 1 to a peak between months 2 and 4, declining steadily to a trough in month 9, then recovering through month 12. The gap between the 1980s and later decades widens notably from month 5 onward, particularly during the summer melt period. Data for all years cover all 12 months except 1987 and 1988, each missing one month, and the current year which is incomplete as of the chart’s publication.
